svg.text: One flat currentColor silhouette. Dark ink ring (1px) + bone halo (1px). Three internal features. Judge at 18 and 32, not at 4x.
4x INSPECTION (80 x 128) -- panel #22223a -- frame1 / frame2 pairs
mechtron -- bob, arms up, right leg steps clear
flapper -- wings spread down, then beat up
leggite -- wave travels down the S, legs scissor
miter joins: the machine keeps hard corners
round joins: 4 left legs, 3 right legs, staggered
This rung is for inspection only. Nothing here was allowed to justify a detail that dies at 32 tall.
3 SPECIES x 4 PLAYER COLORS x 3 BACKGROUNDS, all at 32 tall. Rows top to bottom: mechtron, flapper, leggite. Columns: coral, cyan, green, orchid.
Column 3 of the middle block is the hard case: green player on green terrain. The bone halo, not the ink line, is what cuts it out.
AUCTION DOCK BADGE -- 18 TALL, the smallest rung. Full creature, no crop. All six frames, all four colors.
At this size the faces are gone and that is the design working as intended: identity lives in the silhouette, not in the face.
GREEN ON GREEN: solved by value, not by hue. The bone halo (#f2f0e6) is lighter than both greens and the ink ring (#12121c) is darker than both.
Every creature therefore carries its own contrast with it and needs no per-background special case, no drop shadow, and no outline recolor.
COST, STATED HONESTLY: the double contour spends 2 screen px of the 20px width on each side. That is why there is no internal line work anywhere.
SCALING TO THE OTHER SIX SPECIES: the system is a recipe, not a drawing -- author one silhouette group, get halo, ink, color, and shade for free.
Only the joint style (miter for machines, round for organics) and the three-feature face budget need to be chosen per species.
